title: Simple RAG over CSV Files (Row-Level Ingestion with CSVLoader + FAISS)
one_liner: Turns each CSV row into a retrievable 'column: value' document, embeds it with OpenAI embeddings into a FAISS index, and answers natural-language questions about tabular records with a stuff-documents QA chain.
problem_solved: Standard RAG loaders treat files as running prose, which destroys the row/column structure of tabular data; this technique ingests a CSV so that each record becomes a self-contained, semantically searchable document, enabling plain-English Q&A over structured records (e.g. customer tables) without writing SQL or a custom parser.
failure_modes: My data lives in CSV/spreadsheet files and my RAG pipeline can't answer questions about it
Naive text chunking splits table rows apart so retrieved chunks mix fields from different records
Retrieved chunk contains a value but not the column name, so the LLM can't tell what the number or string means
Users ask lookup questions about a specific record ('which company does X work for?') and free-text retrieval returns unrelated passages
I need a quick natural-language Q&A interface over a small structured dataset without building a text-to-SQL system
when_to_use: Your corpus is one or more CSV files of records (customers, products, tickets) and queries are per-record lookups or descriptions
Each row is short and self-contained, so a single row is a natural retrieval unit
You want a working tabular Q&A prototype in ~30 lines using off-the-shelf LangChain components
Questions reference entity names or attribute values that appear verbatim in the data, so embedding similarity can find the right row
You are new to RAG and want the canonical minimal pipeline (load -> embed -> FAISS -> retrieve -> stuff -> answer) applied to structured data
tradeoffs: Cannot do aggregation, filtering, sorting, or counting ('how many customers joined in 2021?') — retrieval only surfaces top-k rows, so analytical queries need text-to-SQL or a pandas/dataframe agent instead
Embedding every row costs one OpenAI embedding call per row and scales linearly with table size; large tables inflate index build cost and may bury the right row among near-duplicate records
Flat L2 FAISS index (IndexFlatL2) is exact but brute-force; fine for hundreds/thousands of rows, wrong choice for millions
Default retriever settings (top-k, no score threshold) and no reranking: multi-row or fuzzy questions easily retrieve the wrong records
Numeric and date semantics are handled poorly by text embeddings — 'customers subscribed after 2020' will not reliably match rows
In-memory docstore and index: no persistence story out of the box
Do NOT use when the table is wide/huge, when queries are analytical rather than lookup-style, or when exact-match filtering (IDs, emails) matters — a database query is cheaper and correct
how_it_works: The pipeline is a minimal end-to-end RAG system specialized for tabular input. (1) Indexing (dominant stage): pandas previews the CSV, then LangChain's CSVLoader.load_and_split() converts every row into a Document whose page_content is a newline-separated list of 'column: value' pairs — preserving the field-name-to-value binding that naive chunking would lose. (2) A FAISS vector store is built explicitly: OpenAIEmbeddings sizes an IndexFlatL2 (exact L2 search) via a probe embed_query call, wrapped with an InMemoryDocstore, and the row-documents are added with add_documents. (3) Retrieval: vector_store.as_retriever() with default settings fetches the most similar row-documents for a query. (4) Generation: create_stuff_documents_chain packs retrieved rows into a concise-answer system prompt ('say you don't know', max three sentences) for ChatOpenAI gpt-3.5-turbo-0125, and create_retrieval_chain wires retriever and QA chain together. A sample query ('which company does sheryl Baxter work for?') is answered from the retrieved customer row. The technique's novelty is entirely in the ingestion/indexing choice — retrieval and generation are the stock simple-RAG pattern.
key_dependencies: langchain / langchain-community (CSVLoader, FAISS vector store wrapper, InMemoryDocstore, chains)
related_techniques: simple_rag: unstructured-document counterpart — same pipeline over PDF/text instead of CSV rows
simple_csv_rag_with_llamaindex: alternative — identical technique implemented with LlamaIndex instead of LangChain
json_rag: sibling — same 'RAG over structured data' family for JSON instead of CSV
choose_chunk_size: refines — chunking granularity tuning is moot here because the row is the natural unit, but relevant if rows are long
reranking: composes — a reranker over retrieved rows helps when many near-duplicate records exist
reliable_rag: refines — adds relevance checking/grounding on top of this foundational pipeline
summary: Foundational technique for doing RAG over tabular CSV data. Instead of treating a CSV as raw text (where generic chunking splits rows apart and separates values from their column names), LangChain's CSVLoader converts every row into its own Document of 'column: value' lines, preserving record structure and field semantics. Rows are embedded with OpenAIEmbeddings into an exact-search FAISS IndexFlatL2 with an in-memory docstore, exposed via as_retriever(), and answered by a stuff-documents chain (create_retrieval_chain + create_stuff_documents_chain) on gpt-3.5-turbo with a concise 'say you don't know' prompt. Solves per-record lookup questions ('which company does Sheryl Baxter work for?') over datasets like customer tables without SQL. It is strictly a semantic-lookup approach: it cannot aggregate, count, filter by numeric/date ranges, or join — for analytical queries prefer text-to-SQL or a dataframe agent. Costs are one embedding call per row and a brute-force index, fine for small-to-medium tables, poor for millions of rows or near-duplicate records. Best as a starting point that later composes with reranking, retrieval filtering, or reliability checks.
notes_for_chunking: Small notebook (~20 cells), roughly 50/50 markdown/code with short cells. Cell 0 is a promotional book ad (strip). Cell 1 is a large, well-structured overview markdown with '##' headers (Overview, Key Components, Method Details, Benefits, Conclusion) — the highest-value prose, self-contained. Subsequent section headers are inconsistent: some are terse lowercase fragments with typos ('import libries', 'load and process csv data'), others are '#' headers; header quality is too weak to rely on alone, so pair each markdown cell with its following code cell as one chunk. Junk to strip: pip install cell, a wget download cell that also fetches an unrelated PDF (copy-paste artifact from the simple_rag notebook), the pandas .head() output table (medium-size text output), and a final tracking-pixel markdown image cell. No base64 blobs. Code cells are individually meaningful (loader, vector store init, chain assembly, query) and map 1:1 to pipeline stages, making stage-per-chunk a natural split.
